purpose: Identifies the current phase of the inflation cycle and predicts likely sequencing of resolution, including where markets systematically err
components: Phase 1: Goods disinflation from supply chain normalization, inventory restocking, and energy mean-reversion (typically 12-18 months)
Phase 2: Stall zone where goods disinflation exhausts and services persistence becomes visible; markets declare 'mission accomplished' in 4/6 historical episodes
Phase 3: Resolution path — recession-driven (5/5 historical precedent for services), productivity revolution (long-dated), or failure to resolve with second wave
Conditional second-wave probability: accommodative policy during stall → second wave 3/3; restrictive → 0/2
Key signpost: core CPI printing below 2.5% on OER drag is the '1995 trap'
current_assessment: Economy is in late Phase 2 / early Phase 3. Goods disinflation essentially complete. Shelter OER lag will provide 40-80bp mechanical relief through late 2026. Services ex-shelter at 4-5% is the binding constraint. The conditional second-wave base rate (3/3 with accommodative policy) is the most actionable finding for policy monitoring.
limitations: Based on only 6 historical episodes, limiting statistical power
Unique combination of mortgage lock-in, AI, deglobalization, and fiscal expansion means no single episode fully applies
Phase 3 resolution mechanism is the key unknown with no precedent for non-recessionary services resolution

purpose: Quantifies the fundamental driver of services inflation persistence through unit labor costs and identifies conditions required for resolution
components: Services wage growth at 4.0-4.5% as the primary input
Services productivity growth at 0.5-1.0%
Unit labor cost growth = wage growth minus productivity = 3.0-4.0%
Calvo pricing mechanism transmitting ULC to services CPI at 3.5-4.0%
Three resolution channels: wage deceleration (labor market softening), productivity acceleration (AI timeline), margin compression (limited scope)
current_assessment: Services ULC growth at 3.0-4.0% fully explains current persistence without appeal to structural breaks. None of the three resolution channels are operating at sufficient scale within the 2026-2028 horizon. The model correctly predicts the level but does not explain why transmission is slower this cycle.
limitations: Calvo pricing assumption may not capture full heterogeneity of services sector pricing
Does not account for transmission impairment or credit channel delay
Productivity measurement in services is notoriously difficult
Aggregate model may miss sector-specific dynamics (PE consolidation, insurance)

purpose: Assesses wage-price spiral risk by requiring all necessary institutional conditions to be met simultaneously before declaring spiral risk elevated
components: Condition A: Unemployment sustained below ~3.8%
Condition B: Wage growth exceeding 5%
Condition C: Firm pricing power sufficient for full cost pass-through
Condition D: Expectations de-anchoring above Coibion-Gorodnichenko threshold
Institutional prerequisites: union density >25%, COLA coverage >60%
Historical validation: true spirals only in 1966-69 and 1973-75 when all conditions met
current_assessment: Currently 0/3-4 conditions met. Union density ~10%, COLA coverage <10%. The 2021-23 natural experiment (2/3 conditions temporarily met) confirmed the conjunctive requirement. Spiral probability <10-15%. However, inflation can persist through non-spiral channels.
limitations: Does not address non-spiral persistence channels (fiscal, commodity, shelter, expectations drift)
Absence of spiral risk does not imply inflation returns to target
Institutional prerequisites may evolve over long horizons

purpose: Identifies mutually contradictory inflation-path assumptions embedded across asset classes to find tradeable mispricings and determine the dominant portfolio construction variable
components: Equity multiples and implied inflation/discount rate path
Credit spreads (IG OAS 90-110bp, HY 300-350bp) and embedded disinflationary probability (65-70%)
Breakeven inflation levels and risk premium
Real yields and implied equilibrium rate
Maturity-dependent correlation bifurcation (2Y negative, 30Y positive) as fiscal-dominance fingerprint
Non-default spread component as regime indicator (expanded from 60-70% to 75-85% of IG OAS)
current_assessment: Asset classes embed contradictory assumptions. Credit spreads price a disinflationary glide path inconsistent with structural persistence. Breakevens underprice right-tail risk. Inflation optionality offers asymmetric risk/reward regardless of point-estimate debates. Inflation regime identification is the dominant portfolio construction variable.
limitations: TIPS liquidity premium uncertainty (-80bp to +40bp) makes breakeven level claims fragile
Scenario probability weights are inherently subjective (Brier scores of 0.3-0.4 at 12+ month horizons)
Cross-asset trades require the regime to resolve within the trade horizon
Specific alpha estimates do not survive scrutiny even when directional finding does

purpose: Explains why this cycle's inflation is structurally slower to resolve than historical episodes by mapping impaired monetary transmission channels
components: Interest rate channel at ~60% effectiveness
Mortgage lock-in effect freezing housing turnover and insulating household balance sheets
Equity wealth channel operating in reverse (amplifying demand)
Covenant-lite credit structures (>90%) delaying default channel by 12-24 months
CLO plumbing reducing marginal credit market discipline
Fiscal channel (6-7% GDP deficits) partially offsetting tightening
Aggregate: 525bp hikes producing only ~100bp FCI tightening
current_assessment: Multiple transmission channels impaired simultaneously, creating a historically distinct regime. Services inflation is a labor cost phenomenon (NKPC) operating through an impaired transmission mechanism (rate channel) with delayed credit-channel feedback (covenant-lite). Whether monetary policy can resolve this without recession remains the central unresolved question.
limitations: Difficult to separate transmission impairment from the hypothesis that policy simply has not been restrictive enough
Degree of impairment is measured residually with wide confidence intervals
Transmission impairment may self-correct as mortgage lock-in erodes over time
525bp/100bp ratio may overstate impairment if supply normalization did significant independent work

purpose: Decomposes above-target inflation into structural (policy-resistant) and cyclical (policy-responsive) components to determine appropriate policy response
components: Structural components: housing supply deficit (10-20bp), deglobalization/tariffs (10-20bp), healthcare/insurance (10-15bp), energy transition (5-15bp)
Cyclical components: residual labor tightness, fiscal impulse, post-pandemic demand normalization
Methodologies: Beveridge-Nelson, UC-SV, analogue-weighted decomposition
Agent estimates: rates_strategist_02 at 60-70% structural, macro_strategist_01 at 30-40% structural, generalist_02 at ~40% structural
current_assessment: Estimates range from 30-70% structural — the two most methodologically rigorous agents reach nearly opposite conclusions. The divergence stems from classification choices: housing supply deficit as structural vs. labor tightness as cyclical. This is the highest-priority question for future research as it determines whether the inflation regime is amenable to conventional policy or requires structural reform.
limitations: Wide confidence intervals (+/-20-30bp on each component)
Classification of factors as structural vs. cyclical is itself debatable
Different methodologies yield materially different results
Overconfidence in point estimates is the primary epistemic risk
